news_summary: Innovative recycling of old bullets is advancing solar cell technology, while new insights reveal unexpected patterns in U.S. state warming trends.
news_summary_fr: Un procédé innovant de recyclage des vieilles balles fait progresser la technologie des cellules solaires, tandis que de nouvelles découvertes révèlent des tendances inattendues dans le réchauffement climatique des États américains.
news_summary_es: El innovador reciclaje de balas usadas está impulsando el avance de la tecnología de las células solares, mientras que nuevos hallazgos revelan patrones inesperados en las tendencias de calentamiento de los estados de EE. UU.

articles.title: These advanced solar cells have an antique source: old bullets
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01001-0Lead recycled from centuries-old ammunition provides raw materials for perovskite solar cells.
articles.content: Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a sustainable innovation where lead from centuries-old ammunition is recycled to create raw materials for perovskite solar cells, representing a meaningful advancement in renewable energy technology with environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: Old bullets recycled to make advanced solar cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solar cells are devices that turn sunlight into electricity. Scientists are always looking for better materials to make them more efficient and less harmful to the environment.

**What happened** – Researchers found a way to recycle lead from old bullets, some hundreds of years old, to create a key material used in perovskite solar cells. These cells are a new type of solar technology that can be cheaper and more efficient than traditional ones.

**Impact** – This recycling method helps reduce waste from old ammunition and lowers the need to mine new lead, which can harm nature. Using recycled lead in solar cells also supports cleaner energy by making solar technology more sustainable.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on improving this recycling process and testing the solar cells made with recycled lead to see how well they perform in real life. If successful, this could lead to more eco-friendly solar panels in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are turning lead from old bullets into materials for better solar cells, combining recycling with clean energy technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: D'anciennes balles recyclées pour fabriquer des cellules solaires avancées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires transforment la lumière du soleil en électricité. Les scientifiques cherchent toujours des matériaux meilleurs et plus respectueux de l'environnement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont recyclé du plomb provenant de vieilles balles, parfois vieilles de plusieurs siècles, pour fabriquer un matériau important des cellules solaires à pérovskite. Ces cellules sont une nouvelle technologie solaire, souvent moins chère et plus efficace.

**Impact** – Ce recyclage réduit les déchets d’anciennes munitions et évite l’extraction de nouveau plomb, qui peut polluer la nature. Utiliser du plomb recyclé dans les cellules solaires aide aussi à produire une énergie plus propre.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont améliorer ce procédé de recyclage et tester les cellules solaires faites avec ce plomb pour vérifier leur performance. Si ça marche bien, on pourrait avoir des panneaux solaires plus écologiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques transforment le plomb d’anciennes balles en matériaux pour des cellules solaires plus performantes, alliant recyclage et énergie propre.
articles.personality_title_es: Balas antiguas recicladas para fabricar células solares avanzadas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares convierten la luz del sol en electricidad. Los científicos buscan materiales mejores y menos dañinos para el medio ambiente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores reciclaron plomo de balas viejas, algunas con cientos de años, para crear un material clave en las células solares de perovskita. Estas células son una tecnología nueva que puede ser más barata y eficiente.

**Impacto** – Este método reduce los desechos de municiones antiguas y evita la extracción de plomo nuevo, que puede dañar la naturaleza. Usar plomo reciclado ayuda a que la energía solar sea más sostenible.

**Próximos pasos** – Los científicos mejorarán este proceso de reciclaje y probarán las células solares con plomo reciclado para ver cómo funcionan en la práctica. Si funcionan bien, podrían usarse paneles solares más ecológicos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos convierten plomo de balas antiguas en materiales para mejores células solares, uniendo reciclaje y energía limpia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing, translucent solar cells shaped like old bullet casings gently transforming into radiant panels under soft sunlight, set against a simple, natural background with earthy tones and subtle light reflections symbolizing renewal and sustainable energy.

articles.title: Most U.S. states are warming but not in the way you think
articles.summary: Warming across the U.S. is far more uneven than it looks at first glance. While only about half of states show rising average temperatures, most are heating up in specific ways—like hotter highs or warmer lows. These hidden shifts vary by region, with the West seeing more extreme heat and the North losing cold extremes. The findings suggest climate change is playing out differently depending on where you live.
articles.content:
Climate change is affecting the United States in very different ways depending on the region, according to a study published in PLOS Climate. Researchers María Dolores Gadea Rivas of the University of Zaragoza, Spain and Jesús Gonzalo of University Carlos III, Spain found that warming patterns vary widely across the country, pointing to the need for location-specific responses.

Although climate change is a global problem, its impacts are not evenly distributed. Local conditions shape how warming is experienced, which means policies and adaptation strategies must be tailored to specific areas. Compared to topics like public health or economic inequality, these regional climate differences have not been studied in as much detail.

To address this gap, the researchers developed a framework to examine temperature changes across the contiguous 48 United States, allowing for a more precise comparison of how warming unfolds in different locations.

Data Reveals Hidden Warming Trends

The study analyzed average temperatures from 1950 to 2021, along with more than 26,000 daily temperature readings per state. This approach captured not just average changes but the full range of temperatures experienced locally.

The findings show a more complex picture than simple averages suggest. Only 27 states (55%) recorded a rise in average temperatures. However, 41 states (84%) showed increases in at least part of their temperature range. In some places, this means hotter peak temperatures, while in others it reflects milder lows. For instance, states along the West Coast are seeing higher annual temperature extremes, while many northern states are experiencing warmer minimum temperatures.

Impacts on Health, Agriculture, and Policy

These differences could have significant consequences. Shifts in temperature extremes can affect crop growth, strain public health systems, and influence how communities perceive climate risks. All of these factors play an important role in shaping local climate policies and responses.

The researchers note that their method could also be used to study other climate-related changes, including shifts in precipitation patterns and rising sea levels.

Looking Beyond Averages

The authors summarize: "Looking beyond average temperatures, we show that most U.S. states are warming in specific parts of the temperature distribution, even when average warming is not statistically significant. This reveals strong regional inequalities in how climate change is experienced across the United States."

Funding: Gobierno de Aragón and European Regional Development Fund (ERDF, EU), grant LMP71-18 -- received by MDG. Agencia Española de Investigación (MCIN/AEI/10.13039/501100011033) and ERDF, grants PID2020-114646RB-C44 (MDG), PID2023-147593NB-I00 (JG), PID2023-150095NB-C44 (MDG), RED2022-134122-T (LG, JG). MCIN/AEI and European Union NextGenerationEU/PRTR, grant TED2021-129784B-I00 -- received by MDG and JG. MCIN/AEI, grant CEX2021-001181 (María de Maeztu) -- received by JG. Comunidad de Madrid, grants EPUC3M11 and V PRICIT -- received by JG. The funders had no role in study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study revealing detailed, region-specific impacts of climate change across the U.S., highlighting the complexity of warming patterns and the need for tailored local responses. This contributes to better-informed climate policies and adaptation strategies, which have broad societal and environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: Most U.S. states are warming in different ways, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Climate change is a global issue, but its effects are not the same everywhere. In the United States, different regions experience warming in unique ways, which can affect how people and environments adapt.

**What happened** – Researchers from Spain studied temperature data from 1950 to 2021 across the 48 connected U.S. states. They looked beyond average temperatures to see how highs and lows changed. They found that while only about half the states showed a rise in average temperature, most states had warming in some parts of their daily temperature range, such as hotter days or warmer nights.

**Impact** – This study reveals that climate change does not affect all areas equally. For example, the West Coast faces more extreme heat, while northern states have fewer very cold days. These differences matter because they influence farming, health, and how communities prepare for climate risks.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope this detailed approach will help create better local plans to handle climate change. The method could also be used to study other climate changes like rainfall or sea level rise.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Most U.S. states are warming, but in different ways that require local understanding and action.
articles.personality_title_fr: La plupart des États-Unis se réchauffent de façons différentes, révèle une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le changement climatique est un problème mondial, mais ses effets varient selon les régions. Aux États-Unis, chaque zone connaît un réchauffement spécifique qui influence la manière dont les gens et la nature s'adaptent.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs espagnols ont étudié les données de température de 1950 à 2021 dans les 48 États contigus. Ils ont examiné non seulement les températures moyennes, mais aussi les variations des températures maximales et minimales. Ils ont découvert que si seulement la moitié des États montrent une hausse de la température moyenne, la plupart présentent un réchauffement dans certaines parties de leur plage de température quotidienne.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que le changement climatique n'affecte pas toutes les régions de la même façon. Par exemple, la côte Ouest subit des vagues de chaleur plus intenses, tandis que le Nord connaît moins de jours très froids. Ces différences influencent l’agriculture, la santé et la préparation des communautés aux risques climatiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent que cette méthode aidera à élaborer des plans locaux plus adaptés au changement climatique. Elle pourrait aussi servir à étudier d’autres changements comme les précipitations ou la montée des eaux.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La plupart des États-Unis se réchauffent, mais de manières différentes qui demandent une compréhension et une action locales.
articles.personality_title_es: La mayoría de los estados de EE.UU. se calientan de formas diferentes, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cambio climático es un problema global, pero sus efectos no son iguales en todas partes. En Estados Unidos, cada región experimenta el calentamiento de manera diferente, lo que afecta cómo personas y ambientes se adaptan.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de España analizaron datos de temperatura desde 1950 hasta 2021 en los 48 estados contiguos. No solo miraron las temperaturas promedio, sino también cómo cambiaron los máximos y mínimos diarios. Descubrieron que aunque solo la mitad de los estados mostraron aumento en la temperatura promedio, la mayoría tuvo un calentamiento en alguna parte de su rango diario, como días más calientes o noches más cálidas.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que el cambio climático no afecta a todas las áreas por igual. Por ejemplo, la costa Oeste enfrenta más olas de calor extremas, mientras que los estados del Norte tienen menos días muy fríos. Estas diferencias son importantes porque influyen en la agricultura, la salud y la preparación ante riesgos climáticos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan que este enfoque ayude a crear planes locales mejores para enfrentar el cambio climático. También podría usarse para estudiar otros cambios climáticos como las lluvias o el aumento del nivel del mar.

**Resumen en una frase** – La mayoría de los estados de EE.UU. se están calentando, pero de formas distintas que requieren comprensión y acción local.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a stylized map of the contiguous United States made of softly glowing temperature gradients, showing varied warm color zones that highlight shifting temperature extremes and milder lows across different regions, with gentle abstract waves and layered patterns symbolizing diverse local climate changes and the complexity of regional warming trends.
